title: Spectrum, Propagation & Modulation Fundamentals
summary: How radio energy occupies frequency bands, travels by ground-wave, skywave, and line-of-sight, and how information is impressed onto carriers through modulation schemes used in embassy communications.
content:
The radio spectrum is organized by frequency (cycles per second, in Hz) and the inversely related wavelength (lambda = c / f, where c is the speed of light, ~300,000,000 m/s). Higher frequency means shorter wavelength. A diplomatic technology officer must reason about bands because the band dictates antenna size, propagation behavior, and which mission a circuit can serve.
• Key bands: HF (3-30 MHz) for long-haul/regional and beyond-line-of-sight; VHF (30-300 MHz) for local handheld and base operations; UHF (300 MHz-3 GHz) for compound/building penetration and trunked systems; SHF (3-30 GHz) for satellite and point-to-point microwave links.

Propagation modes determine reach. Ground-wave follows the Earth's curvature and dominates at low HF and below, giving reliable short-to-medium range. Skywave is the workhorse of HF embassy nets: signals refract off the ionosphere and return to Earth, enabling intercontinental contact without infrastructure. NVIS (Near Vertical Incidence Skywave) fires HF energy nearly straight up so it rains back down over a 0-300 km radius, filling the skip zone for regional comms in mountainous or denied terrain.

Ionospheric behavior is time- and sun-dependent. Use higher frequencies by day and lower frequencies at night; the MUF (Maximum Usable Frequency) and LUF (Lowest Usable Frequency) bracket the workable window. Solar activity, the 11-year sunspot cycle, and seasonal changes all shift the usable HF spectrum, so frequency plans rotate by time of day.

VHF/UHF and above are essentially line-of-sight (LOS). The radio horizon extends slightly beyond the visual horizon due to atmospheric refraction; a useful approximation is d(km) ~ 4.12 x (sqrt(h_tx) + sqrt(h_rx)) with antenna heights in meters. Raising antennas, not just power, is the most effective way to extend LOS range.

Real channels degrade signals. Multipath occurs when reflections arrive at different times, causing fading and distortion; mobile reception drops into nulls as the unit moves. Every receiver also sits above a noise floor (thermal noise plus man-made interference). What matters for intelligibility is SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio): the margin between wanted signal and noise. Embassy sites with heavy electrical/industrial noise raise the floor and shrink usable range.

Modulation impresses information on a carrier. AM (amplitude modulation) varies amplitude and is simple but noise-prone; FM (frequency modulation) varies frequency, rejects amplitude noise, and gives cleaner audio above a capture threshold, which is why VHF/UHF voice nets use FM. SSB (single sideband) suppresses the carrier and one sideband, concentrating all power in one sideband (USB or LSB) for spectrum- and power-efficient HF voice over long haul.

Digital modulation carries data and digital voice. FSK shifts between frequencies (rugged, used in HF data), PSK shifts carrier phase, and QAM combines amplitude and phase to pack many bits per symbol at the cost of needing higher SNR. Channel bandwidth must fit the assigned slot: narrowbanding moved many VHF/UHF land-mobile channels from 25 kHz to 12.5 kHz to double channel capacity, a mandate diplomatic radio managers must honor in coordinated bands.
keyPoints: Wavelength = speed of light / frequency; higher frequency = shorter wavelength = smaller antennas.
HF 3-30 MHz, VHF 30-300 MHz, UHF 300 MHz-3 GHz, SHF 3-30 GHz; each band has distinct propagation.
Ground-wave hugs the Earth (short range); skywave refracts off the ionosphere for long-haul HF.
NVIS uses near-vertical HF to cover the 0-300 km skip zone, ideal for regional/denied terrain.
Operate above the LUF and below the MUF; use higher frequencies by day, lower at night.
VHF/UHF/SHF are line-of-sight; radio horizon ~4.12 x (sqrt h_tx + sqrt h_rx) km; height beats power for range.
Multipath causes fading; SNR (signal vs noise floor), not raw power, governs intelligibility.
FM rejects amplitude noise (local voice); SSB is power/spectrum efficient for long-haul HF voice.
FSK is rugged for HF data, PSK uses phase, QAM packs more bits but needs higher SNR.
Narrowbanding cut land-mobile channels from 25 kHz to 12.5 kHz to double spectrum capacity.

flashcards.front: Formula relating wavelength and frequency
flashcards.back: Wavelength (lambda) = speed of light (c, ~3x10^8 m/s) / frequency (f). Higher frequency = shorter wavelength.

flashcards.front: Frequency ranges of HF, VHF, UHF, SHF
flashcards.back: HF 3-30 MHz; VHF 30-300 MHz; UHF 300 MHz-3 GHz; SHF 3-30 GHz.

flashcards.front: Ground-wave vs skywave propagation
flashcards.back: Ground-wave follows Earth's curvature for short/medium range; skywave refracts off the ionosphere for long-haul (intercontinental) HF.

flashcards.front: What is NVIS and when is it used?
flashcards.back: Near Vertical Incidence Skywave: HF fired nearly straight up returns over a 0-300 km radius, filling the skip zone for regional comms in mountains/denied areas.

flashcards.front: MUF vs LUF
flashcards.back: MUF = Maximum Usable Frequency (upper limit for skywave at a given time); LUF = Lowest Usable Frequency. Operate between them.

flashcards.front: Why use higher HF frequencies by day, lower at night?
flashcards.back: The ionosphere's ionization is higher in daylight, supporting higher MUF; at night it weakens, so lower frequencies propagate better.

flashcards.front: What is the radio horizon and how does it compare to visual horizon?
flashcards.back: The LOS distance for VHF+ signals; slightly beyond the visual horizon due to atmospheric refraction. ~4.12 x (sqrt h_tx + sqrt h_rx) km.

flashcards.front: Best way to extend line-of-sight range?
flashcards.back: Raise antenna height (both ends) rather than increase power; range grows with the square root of height.

flashcards.front: What is multipath and its effect?
flashcards.back: Multiple reflected copies arrive at different times, causing fading, nulls, and distortion, especially for mobile units.

flashcards.front: What determines intelligibility: power or SNR?
flashcards.back: SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio). Raising the noise floor (electrical/industrial interference) shrinks usable range even at high power.

flashcards.front: AM vs FM core difference and use
flashcards.back: AM varies amplitude (simple, noise-prone); FM varies frequency, rejects amplitude noise, and gives clean local VHF/UHF voice above capture threshold.

flashcards.front: What is SSB and why use it on HF?
flashcards.back: Single Sideband suppresses the carrier and one sideband, concentrating power in USB/LSB for spectrum- and power-efficient long-haul HF voice.

flashcards.front: FSK vs PSK vs QAM
flashcards.back: FSK shifts frequency (rugged HF data); PSK shifts carrier phase; QAM combines amplitude+phase to pack more bits but needs higher SNR.

flashcards.front: What is narrowbanding (12.5 kHz)?
flashcards.back: A mandate to move land-mobile channels from 25 kHz to 12.5 kHz spacing, doubling channel capacity in coordinated VHF/UHF bands.

title: Antennas, Feedline & Network Architecture
summary: Physical RF plant (antennas, feedline, connectors, grounding) and the network topologies (simplex/duplex, repeaters, trunking, squelch) that make embassy radio nets work reliably.
content:
An antenna converts transmitter power into radiated waves and is often the single biggest factor in link performance. Polarization (vertical or horizontal orientation of the wave's electric field) must match between transmit and receive antennas; a mismatch causes large losses. Mobile/handheld VHF-UHF nets are typically vertically polarized.
• Omnidirectional antennas radiate equally in all horizontal directions (good for base stations and roving handhelds); directional antennas such as the Yagi focus energy into a beam for point-to-point links, repeater shots, and long-haul HF.

Gain measures how much an antenna concentrates energy in a desired direction, expressed in dBi (vs an isotropic radiator) or dBd (vs a dipole; add 2.15 dB to convert dBd to dBi). Gain does not create power; it redistributes it, so a high-gain directional antenna increases effective range at the expense of coverage angle.

The feedline carries RF between radio and antenna and must match impedance, almost universally 50 ohms. A mismatch produces reflected power measured as VSWR (Voltage Standing Wave Ratio); 1.0:1 is perfect, and operators generally keep VSWR below about 1.5:1 to 2.0:1 to protect the transmitter and avoid wasted power. Coax also has inherent loss that rises with frequency and length, so UHF/SHF runs demand low-loss cable kept as short as possible.

Connectors must suit the band and environment: N connectors are weatherproof and low-loss for base/outdoor and higher-frequency use; BNC is a quick bayonet connector for test gear and lower-power VHF; SMA is the small threaded connector common on handhelds and modern equipment. A dummy load is a 50-ohm non-radiating resistor used to test or tune a transmitter without putting a signal on the air, important for OPSEC and for not interfering during setup.

Radiated output is quantified as ERP (Effective Radiated Power, referenced to a dipole) and EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power, referenced to isotropic). Both combine transmitter power, feedline loss, and antenna gain; license and coordination limits are usually stated in ERP/EIRP. Proper grounding and lightning protection (ground rods, bonding, surge arrestors/polyphasers on feedlines) protect personnel and equipment at exposed embassy rooftop installations.

Network topology defines who can talk when. Simplex uses one frequency for both directions, so only one party transmits at a time (push-to-talk, PTT). Half-duplex also uses PTT but typically two frequencies; full duplex allows simultaneous talk/listen on separate frequencies like a telephone. PTT discipline is fundamental radio operating procedure.

Repeaters extend coverage by receiving on one frequency and simultaneously retransmitting on another from a high site; the gap between input and output is the offset (e.g., a standard VHF offset of 600 kHz, UHF 5 MHz). Trunked radio systems pool many channels and use a control channel to dynamically assign a free frequency to each call, maximizing capacity for large user populations like a multi-agency mission. Squelch tones keep nets quiet and separated: CTCSS (analog sub-audible tone, also called PL) and DCS (digital coded squelch) cause a receiver to open only for traffic carrying the correct tone/code, blocking other users sharing the channel.
keyPoints: Polarization (vertical/horizontal) must match between ends; mismatch causes major loss. Mobile nets are usually vertical.
Omnidirectional antennas cover all directions (base/handheld); directional Yagi antennas focus a beam for point-to-point.
Gain (dBi vs isotropic, dBd vs dipole; +2.15 dB converts dBd to dBi) redistributes power, trading coverage for range.
RF systems are 50 ohms; VSWR measures impedance mismatch (1.0:1 ideal, keep below ~1.5-2.0:1).
Coax loss increases with frequency and length; keep UHF/SHF runs short with low-loss cable.
Connectors: N (weatherproof, low-loss, high-freq), BNC (bayonet, test/VHF), SMA (small threaded, handhelds).
A dummy load is a 50-ohm non-radiating resistor for testing/tuning without transmitting on the air.
ERP (vs dipole) and EIRP (vs isotropic) combine TX power, feedline loss, and antenna gain; coordination limits use these.
Simplex = one frequency, PTT, one talker; full duplex = simultaneous talk/listen on two frequencies.
Repeaters retransmit from a high site using an offset; trunking uses a control channel to assign free channels dynamically; CTCSS/DCS squelch opens the receiver only for matching traffic.

flashcards.front: Why must antenna polarization match?
flashcards.back: Cross-polarization between TX and RX causes large signal loss (often 20+ dB). Mobile/handheld VHF-UHF nets use vertical polarization.

flashcards.front: Omnidirectional vs directional (Yagi) antenna
flashcards.back: Omni radiates equally in all horizontal directions (base/roving); a directional Yagi focuses energy into a beam for point-to-point and repeater links.

flashcards.front: dBi vs dBd
flashcards.back: dBi = gain vs an isotropic radiator; dBd = gain vs a dipole. Add 2.15 dB to dBd to get dBi.

flashcards.front: Does antenna gain create power?
flashcards.back: No. Gain redistributes the same power into a preferred direction, increasing range there while reducing coverage elsewhere.

flashcards.front: Standard RF system impedance
flashcards.back: 50 ohms for transmitters, feedline, and antennas. Mismatch causes reflected power.

flashcards.front: What is VSWR and an acceptable value?
flashcards.back: Voltage Standing Wave Ratio measures impedance mismatch. 1.0:1 is perfect; operators keep it below ~1.5:1 to 2.0:1 to protect the transmitter.

flashcards.front: How does coax loss change with frequency and length?
flashcards.back: Loss increases with both higher frequency and longer cable, so UHF/SHF runs need short, low-loss coax.

flashcards.front: N vs BNC vs SMA connectors
flashcards.back: N: weatherproof, low-loss, higher-frequency outdoor/base. BNC: quick bayonet for test gear/VHF. SMA: small threaded, common on handhelds.

flashcards.front: What is a dummy load and why use it?
flashcards.back: A 50-ohm non-radiating resistor that absorbs transmitter power for testing/tuning without emitting a signal (OPSEC and non-interference).

flashcards.front: ERP vs EIRP
flashcards.back: ERP is effective radiated power referenced to a dipole; EIRP is referenced to an isotropic source. Both combine TX power, feedline loss, and antenna gain.

flashcards.front: Why is grounding/lightning protection critical?
flashcards.back: Rooftop embassy antennas are exposed; ground rods, bonding, and surge arrestors/polyphasers protect personnel and equipment from strikes and surges.

flashcards.front: Simplex vs half vs full duplex
flashcards.back: Simplex: one frequency, PTT, one talker. Half-duplex: PTT, usually two frequencies. Full duplex: simultaneous talk/listen on separate frequencies.

flashcards.front: How does a repeater work and what is offset?
flashcards.back: It receives on one frequency and simultaneously retransmits on another from a high site. Offset is the input/output gap (e.g., VHF 600 kHz, UHF 5 MHz).

flashcards.front: What is trunked radio and the control channel?
flashcards.back: Many channels are pooled; a dedicated control channel dynamically assigns a free frequency to each call, maximizing capacity for large user groups.

flashcards.front: CTCSS vs DCS
flashcards.back: CTCSS = analog sub-audible tone (PL); DCS = digital coded squelch. Both open the receiver only for traffic carrying the matching tone/code.

title: Secure Comms, Satellite & Diplomatic RF Operations
summary: COMSEC and encryption, secure digital radio standards, satellite options, and the operational discipline (frequency coordination, RF safety, layered backup comms) required for embassy radio operations.
content:
Communications security (COMSEC) protects diplomatic traffic from interception and exploitation, spanning encryption (cryptographic security), transmission security (TRANSEC, hiding the fact and pattern of transmission), emission security (EMSEC), and physical security of keying material. Voice and data are encrypted so that even an intercepted signal yields no intelligible content. Key management is the hard part: keys must be distributed, rotated, and zeroized (securely erased) when compromised or when equipment is at risk of capture.
• OTAR (Over-The-Air Rekeying) lets a control station push new encryption keys to fielded radios over the network, avoiding risky manual key loading and enabling rapid rekey if a key is compromised.

Frequency-hopping spread spectrum (FHSS) rapidly changes the operating frequency in a pseudo-random pattern known only to authorized stations. This provides low probability of intercept/detection (LPI/LPD) and strong jam resistance, valuable in hostile RF environments. Spread-spectrum techniques trade bandwidth for security and robustness.

Digital land-mobile standards dominate secure tactical voice. P25 (Project 25) is the public-safety/government standard: Phase 1 uses 12.5 kHz FDMA channels; Phase 2 uses TDMA to put two voice paths in a 12.5 kHz channel (6.25 kHz equivalent), doubling capacity. DMR (Digital Mobile Radio) is a widely used commercial standard that uses 2-slot TDMA in a 12.5 kHz channel; it is cost-effective but P25 is favored where interoperability with government/public-safety systems and robust encryption are required. Both support built-in AES encryption when configured.

Satellite communications provide reach where terrestrial networks are absent or untrusted. GEO (geostationary, ~35,786 km) satellites appear fixed in the sky, giving wide stable coverage with fixed antennas but a one-way latency of roughly 250 ms (about 500+ ms round trip), noticeable on voice. LEO (low Earth orbit, hundreds of km) constellations give much lower latency and global/polar coverage but require many satellites and tracking/handoff.

Iridium is a LEO constellation offering true global (pole-to-pole) handheld voice and low-rate data, prized for crisis and travel comms in remote regions. INMARSAT operates GEO satellites delivering higher-bandwidth voice and data via larger terminals (BGAN), strong for fixed/semi-fixed embassy use but not covering the extreme poles. VSAT (Very Small Aperture Terminal) is a GEO-based fixed dish providing broadband data/IP trunking to a compound, with the inherent GEO latency to plan around for interactive applications.

Operating legally and safely is part of the job. Frequency coordination requires registering and deconflicting assignments; internationally the ITU allocates spectrum and host-nation authorities must approve diplomatic radio use, often via the foreign ministry. Operating outside coordinated assignments can cause interference and diplomatic friction. RF safety (MPE, maximum permissible exposure) limits human exposure to transmitter energy; keep personnel clear of high-power antennas and observe standoff distances.

Resilience comes from layered, redundant communications. A common planning model is PACE: Primary, Alternate, Contingency, and Emergency paths, each on independent technology (e.g., primary VoIP, alternate VHF repeater, contingency HF, emergency satellite). Emergency and backup comms (HF radio, satphones, batteries/generators, and pre-briefed call plans) ensure the mission can communicate during outages, crises, or evacuation when primary infrastructure fails.
keyPoints: COMSEC spans encryption, TRANSEC, EMSEC, and physical key security; keys must be rotated and zeroized if compromised.
OTAR pushes new encryption keys over the air, avoiding manual loading and enabling rapid rekey.
Frequency-hopping spread spectrum (FHSS) gives LPI/LPD and jam resistance via pseudo-random frequency changes.
P25 Phase 1 = 12.5 kHz FDMA; Phase 2 = TDMA with two voice paths per 12.5 kHz (6.25 kHz equivalent).
DMR uses 2-slot TDMA in 12.5 kHz (cost-effective); P25 is preferred for government interoperability and encryption.
GEO sits at ~35,786 km with ~250 ms one-way latency; LEO is low-altitude with lower latency but needs many satellites.
Iridium = global LEO handheld voice/data (covers poles); INMARSAT = GEO higher-bandwidth terminals (no extreme poles).
VSAT is a GEO fixed dish for compound broadband/IP, with inherent GEO latency to plan around.
Frequency coordination via ITU and host-nation approval is mandatory (uncoordinated use causes interference and friction); RF safety (MPE) requires standoff from high-power antennas.
Use layered redundant comms (PACE: Primary, Alternate, Contingency, Emergency) with independent technologies.

flashcards.front: What does COMSEC cover beyond encryption?
flashcards.back: Cryptographic security, transmission security (TRANSEC), emission security (EMSEC), and physical protection of keying material.

flashcards.front: What is zeroization?
flashcards.back: Securely erasing encryption keys/crypto material from a device, e.g., when a key is compromised or equipment risks capture.

flashcards.front: What is OTAR?
flashcards.back: Over-The-Air Rekeying: a control station pushes new encryption keys to fielded radios over the network, avoiding manual key loading.

flashcards.front: What security benefits does FHSS provide?
flashcards.back: Frequency-hopping spread spectrum gives low probability of intercept/detection (LPI/LPD) and jam resistance via pseudo-random frequency changes.

flashcards.front: P25 Phase 1 vs Phase 2
flashcards.back: Phase 1 = 12.5 kHz FDMA (one voice path); Phase 2 = TDMA fitting two voice paths in 12.5 kHz (6.25 kHz equivalent), doubling capacity.

flashcards.front: DMR vs P25
flashcards.back: DMR: commercial 2-slot TDMA in 12.5 kHz, cost-effective. P25: government/public-safety standard favored for interoperability and robust encryption. Both support AES.

flashcards.front: GEO altitude and latency
flashcards.back: Geostationary at ~35,786 km; appears fixed in the sky but one-way latency is ~250 ms (500+ ms round trip), noticeable on voice.

flashcards.front: LEO vs GEO trade-offs
flashcards.back: LEO (hundreds of km) gives low latency and polar coverage but needs many satellites and handoff; GEO gives stable wide coverage with fixed antennas but high latency.

flashcards.front: Iridium vs INMARSAT
flashcards.back: Iridium: LEO, global pole-to-pole handheld voice/low-rate data. INMARSAT: GEO, higher-bandwidth terminals (BGAN), no coverage at extreme poles.

flashcards.front: What is VSAT?
flashcards.back: Very Small Aperture Terminal: a GEO-based fixed dish providing broadband data/IP trunking to a compound, with inherent GEO latency.

flashcards.front: Who coordinates spectrum internationally and locally?
flashcards.back: The ITU allocates spectrum globally; host-nation authorities (often via the foreign ministry) must approve diplomatic radio use locally.

flashcards.front: What is MPE in RF safety?
flashcards.back: Maximum Permissible Exposure: limits on human exposure to RF energy; keep personnel clear of high-power antennas with proper standoff distances.

flashcards.front: What does PACE stand for?
flashcards.back: Primary, Alternate, Contingency, Emergency: a layered comms plan using independent technologies so a failure of one path is covered by the next.

flashcards.front: Example of layered embassy comms by PACE tier
flashcards.back: Primary: VoIP/internet; Alternate: VHF/UHF repeater; Contingency: HF radio; Emergency: satellite phone (e.g., Iridium).

flashcards.front: Why is uncoordinated transmitting a problem at a post?
flashcards.back: It can interfere with host-nation and other services, violate ITU/host-nation rules, and cause diplomatic friction or loss of operating privileges.
